critique.summary: The video titled 'It Took Me 30 Years to Solve This VFX Problem – Green Screen Problem' by Corridor Crew discusses longstanding challenges in visual effects related to green screen compositing, particularly issues with transparency and chroma keying. The creators explore previous solutions and introduce a new approach involving neural networks and procedural generation to address these problems.
mainPoints: Green screens have inherent problems, especially with transparency and accurate compositing.
Traditional chroma key techniques have limitations that have not fully solved these issues.
The video presents a new solution using neural networks and procedural generation to improve green screen compositing.
The problem has been a persistent challenge in VFX for decades, and this new approach represents a significant advancement.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the difficulties of green screen compositing and chroma key challenges are well-established in the visual effects industry. Corridor Crew is a reputable channel known for detailed VFX explanations and demonstrations. While the video presents a novel approach using neural networks, this aligns with current trends in VFX technology where AI and machine learning are increasingly applied. However, the video is a single source and does not cite multiple independent expert perspectives or peer-reviewed studies, so while the technical claims are plausible and consistent with industry knowledge, independent verification of the new solution's effectiveness is limited.
considerations: The video does not provide external expert validation or comparative benchmarks against other recent VFX technologies.
It does not discuss potential limitations or drawbacks of the neural network-based solution.
The commercial nature of the channel and promotion of merchandise may influence presentation style.
Broader industry adoption and peer-reviewed testing of the new method would strengthen credibility.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the technical challenges and innovations in green screen keying techniques, including the use of synthetic data, lighting issues on set, and hardware requirements. Commenters share insights on alternative methods, historical context, and practical experiences from production environments.

comments.author: dylan604
comments.text:
The sad thing about this is the problems encountered during post from the production team saying "fix it post" during the shoot. I've been on set for green screen shoots where the lighting was not done properly. I watched the gaffer walk across the set taking readings from his meter before saying the lighting was good. I flip on the waveform and told him it was not even (which never goes down well when camera dept tells the gaffer it's not right). He put up an argument, went back and took measurements again before repeating it was good. I flipped the screen around and showed him where it was obviously not even. A third set of meter readings and he starts adjust lights. Once the footage was in post, the fx team commented about how easy the keys were because of the even lighting.

The problem is that the vast majority of people on set have no clue what is going on in post. To the point, when the budget is big enough, a post supervisor is present on production days to give input so "fixing it in post" is minimized. When there is no budget, you'll see situations just like in the first 30 seconds of TFA's video. A single lamp lighting the background so you can easily see the light falling off and the shadows from wrinkles where the screen was just pulled out of the bag 10 minutes before shooting. People just don't realize how much light a green screen takes. They also fail to have enough space so they can pull the talent far enough off the wall to avoid the green reflecting back onto the talent's skin.

TL;DR They solved something to make post less expensive because they cut corners during production.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, experience-based explanation of common production issues affecting green screen quality, highlighting the importance of proper lighting and on-set awareness.

comments.author: Springtime
comments.text: In an earlier video they made a couple years back about Disney's sodium vapor technique Paul Debevec suggested he was considering creating a dataset using a similar premise: filming enough perfectly masked references to be able to train models to achieve better keying. So it was interesting seeing Corridor tackle this by instead using synthetic data.
comments.analysis: The comment references credible sources and ongoing research efforts related to keying technology, showing awareness of the field's development.

comments.author: diacritical
comments.text:
From ~04:10 till 05:00 they talk about sodium-vapor lights and how Disney has the exclusive rights to use it. From what I read the knowledge on how to make them is a trade secret, so it's not patented. Seems weird that it would be hard to recreate something from the 1950's.

I also wonder how many hours were wasted by people who had to use inferior technology because Disney kept it secret. Cutting out animals and objects from the background 1 frame at a time seems so mindnumbingly boring.
comments.analysis: This comment offers historical context and skepticism about trade secrets, which is plausible given the age of the technology and common industry practices.

comments.author: jayd16
comments.text:
As far as alternatives, I wonder if anyone has tried a screen that cycles through colors in a known sequence. Using this modulating-color screen, it might actually be easier to separate the subject because you get around the "green shirt over green screen" problem. You might even be able to use a time sampling to correct the light cast on the subject from the screen as you would have a full spectrum of response.

I could also imagine using polarized light as the backdrop as well.
comments.analysis: The suggestion of alternative technical approaches to keying is creative and grounded in plausible imaging techniques, though unproven in this context.

comments.author: MrVitaliy
comments.text: Anyone tried using lidar and just cut/measure distance to the object?
comments.analysis: The idea to use lidar for segmentation is technically sound and relevant, though it may have practical limitations not discussed here.

comments.author: vsviridov
comments.text: The community has managed to drastically lower hardware requirements, but so far I think only Nvidia cards are supported, so as an AMD owner I'm still missing out :(
comments.analysis: This comment accurately reflects current hardware compatibility issues common in GPU-accelerated applications.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text: There's still a bug: the glass with water does not distort the checker pattern in the background at 24:12.
comments.analysis: The observation points out a specific visual inconsistency in the video, indicating careful attention to detail.

comments.author: superjan
comments.text: Watched this a few days ago. The video is light on technical details, except maybe that they used CGI to generate training data.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a fair critique of the video's content and confirms a key technical aspect mentioned.

comments.author: IshKebab
comments.text:
Pretty impressive results! Seems like someone has even made a GUI for it: https://github.com/edenaion/EZ-CorridorKey

Still Python unfortunately.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses enthusiasm and provides useful information about available tools, though the mention of Python may reflect personal preference.

comments.author: ralusek
comments.text: I'm a software engineer that, like the vast majority of you, uses AI/agents in my workflow every day. That being said, I have to admit that it feels a little weird to hear someone who does not write code say that they built something, without even mentioning that they had an agent build it (unless I missed that).
comments.analysis: This comment reflects personal perspective on AI usage in development, but does not add technical accuracy to the discussion.

comments.author: Computer0
comments.text: Looking forward to trying it out, 8gb of vram or unified memory required!
comments.analysis: This comment shares a hardware requirement likely based on official or community documentation, but lacks depth.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text: Is it a coincidence that the result is stable between subsequent frames?
comments.analysis: The comment poses a question about the stability of the keying result, indicating curiosity but no definitive insight.

comments.author: comex
comments.text:
See also this video comparing Corridor Key to traditional keyers:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=abNygtFqYR8
comments.analysis: This comment provides a relevant resource link, useful for further understanding but adds no original analysis.
